channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: The benefits of made to measure cupboard doors for B2B projects
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Commercial property owners and developers are increasingly turning to made-to-measure cupboard doors as a way to enhance both functionality and aesthetic appeal in business premises. These bespoke solutions offer distinct advantages over standard off-the-shelf options, particularly when it comes to maximising space efficiency and creating tailored interiors that meet specific operational needs.

Why bespoke storage solutions matter for commercial properties

In commercial settings, every square foot counts. Made-to-measure cupboard doors allow businesses to:

  • Utilise awkward or irregular spaces that standard units wouldn't fit
  • Create seamless storage solutions that complement the overall interior design
  • Improve workflow efficiency with storage tailored to specific business requirements
  • Enhance durability with materials selected for high-traffic environments

For landlords and property investors, these custom solutions can potentially add value to commercial units whilst addressing the practical needs of tenants across various sectors, from hospitality to professional services.

Long-term benefits for property investors

Investing in quality fitted storage solutions could prove advantageous for those managing commercial property portfolios. Well-designed made-to-measure cupboards often:

  • Require less maintenance than standard units due to superior materials and construction
  • Extend the lifespan of storage areas, reducing replacement costs
  • Create a more premium feel that may justify higher rental values
  • Provide flexibility to accommodate different tenant requirements over time

Whilst the initial outlay for bespoke joinery might be higher than mass-produced alternatives, the potential long-term savings and value-add could make it a worthwhile consideration for commercial property owners.

As businesses continue to prioritise efficient use of space and professional presentation, made-to-measure storage solutions are likely to remain an important feature in commercial interior projects. Property investors might consider how these tailored elements could enhance both the functionality and marketability of their assets.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Commercial property owners and developers are increasingly turning to made-to-measure cupboard doors as a way to enhance both functionality and aesthetic appeal in business premises. These bespoke solutions offer distinct advantages over standard off-the-shelf options, particularly when it comes to maximising space efficiency and creating tailored interiors that meet specific operational needs.

Why bespoke storage solutions matter for commercial properties

In commercial settings, every square foot counts. Made-to-measure cupboard doors allow businesses to:

  • Utilise awkward or irregular spaces that standard units wouldn't fit
  • Create seamless storage solutions that complement the overall interior design
  • Improve workflow efficiency with storage tailored to specific business requirements
  • Enhance durability with materials selected for high-traffic environments

For landlords and property investors, these custom solutions can potentially add value to commercial units whilst addressing the practical needs of tenants across various sectors, from hospitality to professional services.

Long-term benefits for property investors

Investing in quality fitted storage solutions could prove advantageous for those managing commercial property portfolios. Well-designed made-to-measure cupboards often:

  • Require less maintenance than standard units due to superior materials and construction
  • Extend the lifespan of storage areas, reducing replacement costs
  • Create a more premium feel that may justify higher rental values
  • Provide flexibility to accommodate different tenant requirements over time

Whilst the initial outlay for bespoke joinery might be higher than mass-produced alternatives, the potential long-term savings and value-add could make it a worthwhile consideration for commercial property owners.

As businesses continue to prioritise efficient use of space and professional presentation, made-to-measure storage solutions are likely to remain an important feature in commercial interior projects. Property investors might consider how these tailored elements could enhance both the functionality and marketability of their assets.

item.title: The Hidden Costs of Buying the Wrong Investment Property: 7 Mistakes Investors Make Before They Buy
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst many property investors focus heavily on rental yields and market trends, some of the most expensive mistakes occur during the initial property search and purchase stages. Overlooking key factors early in the process could lead to costly consequences that only become apparent months or years later. Being aware of common pitfalls might help investors make more informed decisions when building their portfolios.

Common Pre-Purchase Oversights

Investors often concentrate on the financial aspects of property investment whilst underestimating other crucial considerations. Some frequent oversights include:

  • Failing to properly research local rental demand and tenant profiles
  • Underestimating ongoing maintenance costs for older properties
  • Overlooking restrictive covenants or unusual lease terms
  • Not accounting for potential void periods in cash flow calculations
  • Ignoring upcoming local developments that could affect property values

These factors might not immediately impact profitability but could significantly affect long-term returns. Investors might wish to create a comprehensive checklist that goes beyond basic financial metrics when evaluating potential purchases.

Hidden Costs That Can Erode Returns

Some expenses only become apparent after completing a purchase. Unexpected costs that could affect an investment's performance include:

  • Higher-than-anticipated renovation costs to make properties lettable
  • Unexpected service charges or ground rent increases in leasehold properties
  • Compliance costs for new energy efficiency regulations
  • Legal fees for dealing with problematic tenancies or disputes
  • Insurance premiums for properties in flood-risk areas

Conducting thorough due diligence, including detailed property surveys and legal checks, might help identify potential issues before exchange. Investors may also consider building larger financial buffers to account for unforeseen expenses.

Property investment requires careful planning beyond simple yield calculations. By paying attention to often-overlooked factors during the purchasing process, investors might avoid costly mistakes and build more resilient portfolios. Professional advice from qualified mortgage brokers and property specialists could provide valuable insights tailored to individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst many property investors focus heavily on rental yields and market trends, some of the most expensive mistakes occur during the initial property search and purchase stages. Overlooking key factors early in the process could lead to costly consequences that only become apparent months or years later. Being aware of common pitfalls might help investors make more informed decisions when building their portfolios.

Common Pre-Purchase Oversights

Investors often concentrate on the financial aspects of property investment whilst underestimating other crucial considerations. Some frequent oversights include:

  • Failing to properly research local rental demand and tenant profiles
  • Underestimating ongoing maintenance costs for older properties
  • Overlooking restrictive covenants or unusual lease terms
  • Not accounting for potential void periods in cash flow calculations
  • Ignoring upcoming local developments that could affect property values

These factors might not immediately impact profitability but could significantly affect long-term returns. Investors might wish to create a comprehensive checklist that goes beyond basic financial metrics when evaluating potential purchases.

Hidden Costs That Can Erode Returns

Some expenses only become apparent after completing a purchase. Unexpected costs that could affect an investment's performance include:

  • Higher-than-anticipated renovation costs to make properties lettable
  • Unexpected service charges or ground rent increases in leasehold properties
  • Compliance costs for new energy efficiency regulations
  • Legal fees for dealing with problematic tenancies or disputes
  • Insurance premiums for properties in flood-risk areas

Conducting thorough due diligence, including detailed property surveys and legal checks, might help identify potential issues before exchange. Investors may also consider building larger financial buffers to account for unforeseen expenses.

Property investment requires careful planning beyond simple yield calculations. By paying attention to often-overlooked factors during the purchasing process, investors might avoid costly mistakes and build more resilient portfolios. Professional advice from qualified mortgage brokers and property specialists could provide valuable insights tailored to individual circumstances.

item.title: Is the landlord right to claim for cleaning at the end of the tenancy?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlord awarded cleaning costs after tenant dispute

A recent tenancy dispute has highlighted the importance of understanding cleaning obligations when moving out of a rental property. The case involved a landlord claiming £245 for professional cleaning after their tenant vacated, arguing the property hadn't been returned to its original condition.

The cleaning standards debate

Cleaning disagreements remain one of the most common issues at the end of tenancies. This particular case saw differing expectations between landlord and tenant regarding what constitutes an acceptable standard. The tenant maintained they'd left the property in a "reasonable domestic condition", while the landlord expected cleanliness matching the professional standard documented at check-in.

The inventory report from the tenancy start showed:

  • Professional cleaning throughout
  • Spotless kitchen appliances
  • Limescale-free bathroom fittings
  • Freshly vacuumed carpets

At check-out, evidence showed grease in the oven, limescale deposits, dusty skirting boards and carpet stains. The tenant argued some issues like limescale were due to hard water rather than inadequate cleaning.

Key lessons for tenants and landlords

The adjudicator's decision to award the landlord £180 (reduced from the original claim) provides several important reminders:

  • The check-in inventory sets the cleanliness benchmark tenants need to match
  • "Domestic cleaning" may not meet contractual obligations if the property was professionally cleaned initially
  • Photographic evidence from both parties strengthens any claim
  • Tenants might consider professional cleaning if unsure about meeting required standards

This case illustrates how clear documentation and understanding of tenancy agreements can help prevent disputes. Both landlords and tenants may wish to ensure proper records are kept and expectations are communicated clearly from the outset.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlord awarded cleaning costs after tenant dispute

A recent tenancy dispute has highlighted the importance of understanding cleaning obligations when moving out of a rental property. The case involved a landlord claiming £245 for professional cleaning after their tenant vacated, arguing the property hadn't been returned to its original condition.

The cleaning standards debate

Cleaning disagreements remain one of the most common issues at the end of tenancies. This particular case saw differing expectations between landlord and tenant regarding what constitutes an acceptable standard. The tenant maintained they'd left the property in a "reasonable domestic condition", while the landlord expected cleanliness matching the professional standard documented at check-in.

The inventory report from the tenancy start showed:

  • Professional cleaning throughout
  • Spotless kitchen appliances
  • Limescale-free bathroom fittings
  • Freshly vacuumed carpets

At check-out, evidence showed grease in the oven, limescale deposits, dusty skirting boards and carpet stains. The tenant argued some issues like limescale were due to hard water rather than inadequate cleaning.

Key lessons for tenants and landlords

The adjudicator's decision to award the landlord £180 (reduced from the original claim) provides several important reminders:

  • The check-in inventory sets the cleanliness benchmark tenants need to match
  • "Domestic cleaning" may not meet contractual obligations if the property was professionally cleaned initially
  • Photographic evidence from both parties strengthens any claim
  • Tenants might consider professional cleaning if unsure about meeting required standards

This case illustrates how clear documentation and understanding of tenancy agreements can help prevent disputes. Both landlords and tenants may wish to ensure proper records are kept and expectations are communicated clearly from the outset.

item.title: Former Knight Frank directors launch new property consultancy
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Two seasoned professionals from Knight Frank’s land and development team have embarked on a new venture, establishing a property consultancy tailored to the Midlands market. Will Jordan and Charlie Steele, who boast a combined experience of nearly four decades in the residential development sector, have launched Ashlawn Chase. Based in Henley-in-Arden, the firm aims to provide expert advice to landowners, developers, and investors on land acquisitions, disposals, and strategic opportunities.

Expertise and Focus

Ashlawn Chase positions itself as a consultancy offering straightforward, commercially focused advice, with clients gaining direct access to its founders. Jordan and Steele bring a wealth of experience, having previously advised on a variety of projects across the Midlands, including city centre regeneration schemes, residential developments, and mixed-use sites. Their roles at Knight Frank involved significant transactions and advisory work in Birmingham and the wider region, equipping them with a deep understanding of the local market.

The new consultancy arrives at a time when the residential land market remains active yet challenging. Developers and landowners are increasingly seeking specialist guidance to navigate planning hurdles, viability pressures, and evolving market conditions. Ashlawn Chase aims to address these needs by providing a personal and agile service, grounded in strong relationships and a results-driven approach.

A Personalised Approach

One of the key principles behind Ashlawn Chase is the emphasis on direct access to experienced advisers. Jordan, formerly head of development at Knight Frank’s Birmingham office, highlighted the importance of understanding both opportunities and risks in the current market. “We saw an opportunity to create a business that offers straightforward, commercially focused advice without unnecessary complexity,” he said. “Clients increasingly value direct access to experienced advisers who can guide them through the challenges.”

Steele echoed this sentiment, noting that their shared approach to business has always been relationship-led and commercially grounded. “Launching Ashlawn Chase gives us the opportunity to provide a more personal and agile service to clients across the region,” he explained. This client-centric philosophy is expected to set the consultancy apart in a competitive market.

As Ashlawn Chase begins its journey, its founders are well-positioned to support Midlands-based clients in navigating the complexities of the residential land market. With their extensive experience and commitment to delivering tailored solutions, Jordan and Steele aim to make a significant impact in the region’s property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Two seasoned professionals from Knight Frank’s land and development team have embarked on a new venture, establishing a property consultancy tailored to the Midlands market. Will Jordan and Charlie Steele, who boast a combined experience of nearly four decades in the residential development sector, have launched Ashlawn Chase. Based in Henley-in-Arden, the firm aims to provide expert advice to landowners, developers, and investors on land acquisitions, disposals, and strategic opportunities.

Expertise and Focus

Ashlawn Chase positions itself as a consultancy offering straightforward, commercially focused advice, with clients gaining direct access to its founders. Jordan and Steele bring a wealth of experience, having previously advised on a variety of projects across the Midlands, including city centre regeneration schemes, residential developments, and mixed-use sites. Their roles at Knight Frank involved significant transactions and advisory work in Birmingham and the wider region, equipping them with a deep understanding of the local market.

The new consultancy arrives at a time when the residential land market remains active yet challenging. Developers and landowners are increasingly seeking specialist guidance to navigate planning hurdles, viability pressures, and evolving market conditions. Ashlawn Chase aims to address these needs by providing a personal and agile service, grounded in strong relationships and a results-driven approach.

A Personalised Approach

One of the key principles behind Ashlawn Chase is the emphasis on direct access to experienced advisers. Jordan, formerly head of development at Knight Frank’s Birmingham office, highlighted the importance of understanding both opportunities and risks in the current market. “We saw an opportunity to create a business that offers straightforward, commercially focused advice without unnecessary complexity,” he said. “Clients increasingly value direct access to experienced advisers who can guide them through the challenges.”

Steele echoed this sentiment, noting that their shared approach to business has always been relationship-led and commercially grounded. “Launching Ashlawn Chase gives us the opportunity to provide a more personal and agile service to clients across the region,” he explained. This client-centric philosophy is expected to set the consultancy apart in a competitive market.

As Ashlawn Chase begins its journey, its founders are well-positioned to support Midlands-based clients in navigating the complexities of the residential land market. With their extensive experience and commitment to delivering tailored solutions, Jordan and Steele aim to make a significant impact in the region’s property landscape.

item.title: Lights, camera, property! Estate agency rolls out the red carpet for film industry
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agency launches filming locations service for UK productions

Carter Jonas has introduced a specialised filming locations service catering to the television, film and photography industries. The new division aims to connect production companies with suitable properties whilst assisting landlords in generating income from their assets. The service will handle everything from initial viewings to post-filming reinstatement.

The estate agency's extensive property portfolio includes residential homes, rural estates and commercial spaces, providing diverse options for location scouts. With the UK's creative industries continuing to thrive, the service arrives as production companies seek fresh and unusual filming settings across the country.

Opportunities for property owners and production teams

The initiative offers potential benefits for both property owners and filmmakers:

  • Landlords with vacant or underutilised buildings may wish to consider this as an alternative revenue stream
  • Production companies gain access to a curated selection of locations through a single point of contact
  • The service manages licensing agreements and on-site coordination, reducing administrative burdens
  • Specialist locations such as industrial sites and rural estates could prove particularly attractive to location managers

Dan Douglas, heading the new division, brings considerable industry experience having previously worked in film and television location services. His team will focus on matching properties to productions while ensuring minimal disruption to owners.

Alexandra Houghton, Carter Jonas' head of commercial, commented: "Our national reach and property expertise position us ideally to support the creative sector whilst helping clients maximise their assets' potential." Chief executive Richard Bruce added that the service reflects the firm's adaptability in responding to evolving market opportunities.

For homeowners with distinctive properties, this development might present an interesting way to generate additional income whilst contributing to the UK's thriving creative economy.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agency launches filming locations service for UK productions

Carter Jonas has introduced a specialised filming locations service catering to the television, film and photography industries. The new division aims to connect production companies with suitable properties whilst assisting landlords in generating income from their assets. The service will handle everything from initial viewings to post-filming reinstatement.

The estate agency's extensive property portfolio includes residential homes, rural estates and commercial spaces, providing diverse options for location scouts. With the UK's creative industries continuing to thrive, the service arrives as production companies seek fresh and unusual filming settings across the country.

Opportunities for property owners and production teams

  • Landlords with vacant or underutilised buildings may wish to consider this as an alternative revenue stream
  • Production companies gain access to a curated selection of locations through a single point of contact

Dan Douglas, heading the new division, brings considerable industry experience having previously worked in film and television location services. His team will focus on matching properties to productions while ensuring minimal disruption to owners.

Alexandra Houghton, Carter Jonas' head of commercial, commented: "Our national reach and property expertise position us ideally to support the creative sector whilst helping clients maximise their assets' potential." Chief executive Richard Bruce added that the service reflects the firm's adaptability in responding to evolving market opportunities.

For homeowners with distinctive properties, this development might present an interesting way to generate additional income whilst contributing to the UK's thriving creative economy.

item.title: Elite Realty Invest to target double digit growth
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Specialist property investment firm Elite Realty Invest has announced ambitious expansion plans, aiming for double-digit growth by 2026. The company's strategy includes significant operational changes, from workforce expansion to international ventures and securing prime development partnerships.

Expansion plans take shape

Elite Realty Invest has reportedly doubled its workforce in preparation for its growth targets, alongside establishing its first overseas office in Turkey. This international move suggests the firm is looking beyond domestic markets whilst maintaining a strong focus on UK property investment opportunities.

The company has also secured master agent status on several high-end residential developments across the UK. This privileged position gives Elite Realty Invest exclusive access to premium investment properties before they reach the general market, potentially offering clients early opportunities on sought-after developments.

What this means for property investors

For those considering property investment, Elite Realty Invest's expansion could signal several market developments:

  • Increased availability of prime residential properties through exclusive development partnerships
  • Potential for more competitive investment products as firms expand their portfolios
  • Greater international investment options, particularly in emerging markets like Turkey

Property investors might wish to monitor how these expansion plans develop, particularly regarding the types of investment opportunities that may become available through the firm's master agent partnerships. As always, thorough research and independent financial advice remain crucial when considering any property investment.

While Elite Realty Invest's growth ambitions reflect confidence in the property market, market conditions can change rapidly. Investors may find it valuable to keep abreast of both domestic and international property trends when making long-term investment decisions.

Specialist property investment firm Elite Realty Invest has announced ambitious expansion plans, aiming for double-digit growth by 2026. The company's strategy includes significant operational changes, from workforce expansion to international ventures and securing prime development partnerships.

Property investors might wish to monitor how these expansion plans develop, particularly regarding the types of investment opportunities that may become available through the firm's master agent partnerships. As always, thorough research and independent financial advice remain crucial when considering any property investment.

While Elite Realty Invest's growth ambitions reflect confidence in the property market, market conditions can change rapidly. Investors may find it valuable to keep abreast of both domestic and international property trends when making long-term investment decisions.

item.title: 10% rise in sales agreed as housing recovers
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market has shown signs of renewed activity, with a notable 10% increase in agreed sales during April compared to previous months. Industry analysts suggest this uptick may signal the beginning of a broader market recovery, with more accurately priced properties attracting buyer interest across Britain.

Improved pricing strategies boost market activity

Recent data indicates that sellers who have adjusted their pricing expectations in line with current market conditions are seeing greater success. Estate agents report that properties priced realistically from the outset are attracting more viewings and achieving faster sales. This shift towards pricing accuracy appears to be helping to bridge the gap between buyer expectations and seller aspirations.

Several factors may be contributing to this positive trend:

  • Mortgage rates showing signs of stabilisation after recent volatility
  • Increased buyer confidence as the economic outlook improves
  • Seasonal factors typically boosting spring market activity

Regional variations in market performance

Whilst the national picture shows improvement, regional variations remain significant. Areas with stronger employment markets and good transport links continue to outperform more peripheral locations. The Midlands and certain parts of the North West appear to be leading the recovery, with London's market showing more modest gains.

Prospective buyers might consider that properties in popular commuter towns and cities with good schools often maintain stronger demand. Meanwhile, sellers in less sought-after locations may need to be particularly mindful of competitive pricing strategies to attract interest.

The coming months will reveal whether this positive trend represents a sustained recovery or a temporary seasonal boost. For now, the increased sales activity offers cautious optimism for both buyers and sellers navigating the UK property market.

The UK housing market has shown signs of renewed activity, with a notable 10% increase in agreed sales during April compared to previous months. Industry analysts suggest this uptick may signal the beginning of a broader market recovery, with more accurately priced properties attracting buyer interest across Britain.

Improved pricing strategies boost market activity

Recent data indicates that sellers who have adjusted their pricing expectations in line with current market conditions are seeing greater success. Estate agents report that properties priced realistically from the outset are attracting more viewings and achieving faster sales. This shift towards pricing accuracy appears to be helping to bridge the gap between buyer expectations and seller aspirations.

Several factors may be contributing to this positive trend:

  • Mortgage rates showing signs of stabilisation after recent volatility
  • Increased buyer confidence as the economic outlook improves
  • Seasonal factors typically boosting spring market activity

Regional variations in market performance

Whilst the national picture shows improvement, regional variations remain significant. Areas with stronger employment markets and good transport links continue to outperform more peripheral locations. The Midlands and certain parts of the North West appear to be leading the recovery, with London's market showing more modest gains.

Prospective buyers might consider that properties in popular commuter towns and cities with good schools often maintain stronger demand. Meanwhile, sellers in less sought-after locations may need to be particularly mindful of competitive pricing strategies to attract interest.

The coming months will reveal whether this positive trend represents a sustained recovery or a temporary seasonal boost. For now, the increased sales activity offers cautious optimism for both buyers and sellers navigating the UK property market.

item.title: Cyprus emerges as new Med property investment hotspot
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

British property investors appear to be increasingly looking towards Cyprus as a Mediterranean investment destination, with growing interest in the island's residency-through-investment schemes and year-round sunshine appeal. The EU member state has seen a notable uptick in overseas buyer enquiries, particularly from those considering second homes or alternative residency options post-Brexit.

Why Cyprus is attracting international buyers

Several factors make Cyprus stand out in the Mediterranean property market. The island offers:

  • A permanent residency programme requiring a €300,000 property investment
  • No inheritance tax and relatively low property purchase costs compared to other EU nations
  • Over 300 days of sunshine annually and English widely spoken
  • Direct flights from multiple UK airports taking under five hours

Property prices in Cyprus remain below many western European destinations, though prime coastal areas have seen values rise steadily in recent years. The combination of lifestyle benefits and investment potential appears to be drawing particular interest from UK-based buyers.

Considerations for overseas property investment

Whilst Cyprus presents opportunities, prospective buyers might wish to carefully evaluate several aspects:

  • Local property taxes and ongoing maintenance costs differ from UK systems
  • Residency rules require maintaining the qualifying property investment
  • Currency fluctuations between sterling and the euro could affect costs
  • Legal processes follow Cypriot rather than UK property law

Independent financial advice is typically recommended when considering overseas property purchases, particularly where residency rights or significant capital outlays are involved. Many buyers choose to work with both UK-based international mortgage specialists and local Cypriot legal representatives.

For those exploring Mediterranean property options, Cyprus currently represents one of several EU destinations combining lifestyle appeal with potential investment returns. As with any overseas purchase, thorough research and professional guidance remain essential considerations.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

British property investors appear to be increasingly looking towards Cyprus as a Mediterranean investment destination, with growing interest in the island's residency-through-investment schemes and year-round sunshine appeal. The EU member state has seen a notable uptick in overseas buyer enquiries, particularly from those considering second homes or alternative residency options post-Brexit.

Why Cyprus is attracting international buyers

Several factors make Cyprus stand out in the Mediterranean property market. The island offers:

  • A permanent residency programme requiring a €300,000 property investment
  • No inheritance tax and relatively low property purchase costs compared to other EU nations
  • Over 300 days of sunshine annually and English widely spoken
  • Direct flights from multiple UK airports taking under five hours

Property prices in Cyprus remain below many western European destinations, though prime coastal areas have seen values rise steadily in recent years. The combination of lifestyle benefits and investment potential appears to be drawing particular interest from UK-based buyers.

Considerations for overseas property investment

Whilst Cyprus presents opportunities, prospective buyers might wish to carefully evaluate several aspects:

  • Local property taxes and ongoing maintenance costs differ from UK systems
  • Residency rules require maintaining the qualifying property investment
  • Currency fluctuations between sterling and the euro could affect costs
  • Legal processes follow Cypriot rather than UK property law

Independent financial advice is typically recommended when considering overseas property purchases, particularly where residency rights or significant capital outlays are involved. Many buyers choose to work with both UK-based international mortgage specialists and local Cypriot legal representatives.

For those exploring Mediterranean property options, Cyprus currently represents one of several EU destinations combining lifestyle appeal with potential investment returns. As with any overseas purchase, thorough research and professional guidance remain essential considerations.

item.title: Challenger firm aims to overhaul conveyancing
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New Conveyancing Service Launches to Tackle Property Transaction Delays

A fresh face in the conveyancing sector has emerged with ambitions to streamline the often-frustrating property purchase process. The new firm promises to address the chronic delays and communication breakdowns that have plagued homebuyers and sellers in recent years.

Modernising an Outdated System

The property market has seen unprecedented challenges with conveyancing backlogs since the pandemic-induced housing boom. Many homeowners might recall the stress of:

  • Weeks of radio silence from legal teams
  • Missed completion deadlines
  • Chain collapses due to administrative delays

This new entrant aims to overhaul traditional practices by implementing digital solutions and transparent tracking systems. Borrowers considering a property purchase might find such innovations particularly appealing when timing is crucial.

What This Means for Mortgage Applicants

For those navigating the mortgage process, efficient conveyancing can make all the difference between securing a dream home and losing out to competitors. The firm's proposed improvements could potentially:

  • Reduce the risk of mortgage offers expiring due to delays
  • Provide real-time updates on transaction progress
  • Minimise last-minute surprises before completion

Whilst it's early days for the new service, any meaningful improvement in conveyancing could positively impact the entire property purchasing journey. Homeowners preparing to move may wish to research all available options when selecting their legal representation.

As the property market continues to evolve, such innovations highlight the growing demand for more efficient, consumer-focused services throughout the homebuying process.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New Conveyancing Service Launches to Tackle Property Transaction Delays

A fresh face in the conveyancing sector has emerged with ambitions to streamline the often-frustrating property purchase process. The new firm promises to address the chronic delays and communication breakdowns that have plagued homebuyers and sellers in recent years.

Modernising an Outdated System

The property market has seen unprecedented challenges with conveyancing backlogs since the pandemic-induced housing boom. Many homeowners might recall the stress of:

  • Weeks of radio silence from legal teams
  • Missed completion deadlines
  • Chain collapses due to administrative delays

This new entrant aims to overhaul traditional practices by implementing digital solutions and transparent tracking systems. Borrowers considering a property purchase might find such innovations particularly appealing when timing is crucial.

What This Means for Mortgage Applicants

For those navigating the mortgage process, efficient conveyancing can make all the difference between securing a dream home and losing out to competitors. The firm's proposed improvements could potentially:

  • Reduce the risk of mortgage offers expiring due to delays
  • Provide real-time updates on transaction progress
  • Minimise last-minute surprises before completion

Whilst it's early days for the new service, any meaningful improvement in conveyancing could positively impact the entire property purchasing journey. Homeowners preparing to move may wish to research all available options when selecting their legal representation.

As the property market continues to evolve, such innovations highlight the growing demand for more efficient, consumer-focused services throughout the homebuying process.

item.title: IHT liabilities hitting six figures as property values rise
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Growing numbers of UK families could face substantial inheritance tax (IHT) bills as rising property prices push more estates above the tax-free threshold. Recent analysis suggests average IHT liabilities may now exceed £100,000 for many households, with residential property values being a key contributing factor.

How property values affect IHT exposure

The standard IHT nil-rate band has remained frozen at £325,000 since 2009, whilst the additional residence nil-rate band (for passing on main homes to direct descendants) stands at £175,000. With average UK house prices now significantly higher than a decade ago, more estates are being pushed into taxable territory. Properties in London and the South East are particularly likely to trigger IHT charges, where average values frequently exceed both allowances combined.

  • The nil-rate band has been frozen until at least April 2028
  • Properties typically form the largest asset in most estates
  • IHT is charged at 40% on values above the thresholds

Planning options for homeowners

Those concerned about potential IHT liabilities might consider several planning approaches, though professional financial advice is always recommended. Some options include:

  • Making use of annual gifting allowances during one's lifetime
  • Exploring trusts or life insurance policies written in trust
  • Considering equity release to reduce the estate's value
  • Reviewing wills regularly to ensure optimal use of allowances

With property values showing no signs of significant decline and tax thresholds remaining static, early planning could help mitigate future IHT burdens. However, all strategies should be carefully evaluated against individual circumstances and potential implications.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Growing numbers of UK families could face substantial inheritance tax (IHT) bills as rising property prices push more estates above the tax-free threshold. Recent analysis suggests average IHT liabilities may now exceed £100,000 for many households, with residential property values being a key contributing factor.

How property values affect IHT exposure

The standard IHT nil-rate band has remained frozen at £325,000 since 2009, whilst the additional residence nil-rate band (for passing on main homes to direct descendants) stands at £175,000. With average UK house prices now significantly higher than a decade ago, more estates are being pushed into taxable territory. Properties in London and the South East are particularly likely to trigger IHT charges, where average values frequently exceed both allowances combined.

  • The nil-rate band has been frozen until at least April 2028
  • Properties typically form the largest asset in most estates
  • IHT is charged at 40% on values above the thresholds

Planning options for homeowners

Those concerned about potential IHT liabilities might consider several planning approaches, though professional financial advice is always recommended. Some options include:

  • Making use of annual gifting allowances during one's lifetime
  • Exploring trusts or life insurance policies written in trust
  • Considering equity release to reduce the estate's value
  • Reviewing wills regularly to ensure optimal use of allowances

With property values showing no signs of significant decline and tax thresholds remaining static, early planning could help mitigate future IHT burdens. However, all strategies should be carefully evaluated against individual circumstances and potential implications.

item.title: Paragon streamlines further advance and cuts rates for landlords
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Specialist lender Paragon has announced significant changes to its buy-to-let mortgage offerings, introducing rate reductions and streamlining its further advance process. These adjustments come as landlords across England and Wales prepare for upcoming energy efficiency regulations that may require property upgrades.

Enhanced lending options for portfolio landlords

Paragon has reduced rates across its range of further advance products, with cuts of up to 0.45 percentage points. The lender has also simplified its application process for additional borrowing, potentially making it quicker for experienced landlords to access funds for property improvements or portfolio expansion.

These changes may be particularly timely given the anticipated tightening of Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) requirements. From 2025, new tenancies will require a minimum EPC rating of C, extending to all tenancies by 2028. Many landlords might need to undertake energy efficiency improvements to meet these standards.

Preparing for greener rental properties

The lender's product adjustments appear designed to support landlords facing the dual challenges of regulatory changes and current economic conditions. With the EPC deadline approaching, property investors might consider reviewing their portfolios to identify which properties may need upgrades such as improved insulation, modern heating systems or renewable energy installations.

Paragon's streamlined further advance process could help landlords secure funding more efficiently for these essential improvements. The lender has indicated that applications may now be processed within shorter timeframes, though exact timescales will depend on individual circumstances.

Mortgage brokers specialising in buy-to-let finance suggest that landlords planning property upgrades might benefit from comparing both further advance options and remortgage deals. While further advances allow borrowers to access additional funds against their existing properties, remortgaging could potentially secure better terms depending on current equity levels and property values.

As the rental market continues to evolve, specialist lenders like Paragon are adapting their products to meet changing landlord requirements. With energy efficiency standards rising and operating costs increasing, property investors may wish to review their financing options well in advance of regulatory deadlines.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Specialist lender Paragon has announced significant changes to its buy-to-let mortgage offerings, introducing rate reductions and streamlining its further advance process. These adjustments come as landlords across England and Wales prepare for upcoming energy efficiency regulations that may require property upgrades.

Enhanced lending options for portfolio landlords

Paragon has reduced rates across its range of further advance products, with cuts of up to 0.45 percentage points. The lender has also simplified its application process for additional borrowing, potentially making it quicker for experienced landlords to access funds for property improvements or portfolio expansion.

These changes may be particularly timely given the anticipated tightening of Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) requirements. From 2025, new tenancies will require a minimum EPC rating of C, extending to all tenancies by 2028. Many landlords might need to undertake energy efficiency improvements to meet these standards.

Preparing for greener rental properties

The lender's product adjustments appear designed to support landlords facing the dual challenges of regulatory changes and current economic conditions. With the EPC deadline approaching, property investors might consider reviewing their portfolios to identify which properties may need upgrades such as improved insulation, modern heating systems or renewable energy installations.

Paragon's streamlined further advance process could help landlords secure funding more efficiently for these essential improvements. The lender has indicated that applications may now be processed within shorter timeframes, though exact timescales will depend on individual circumstances.

Mortgage brokers specialising in buy-to-let finance suggest that landlords planning property upgrades might benefit from comparing both further advance options and remortgage deals. While further advances allow borrowers to access additional funds against their existing properties, remortgaging could potentially secure better terms depending on current equity levels and property values.

As the rental market continues to evolve, specialist lenders like Paragon are adapting their products to meet changing landlord requirements. With energy efficiency standards rising and operating costs increasing, property investors may wish to review their financing options well in advance of regulatory deadlines.

item.title: Lettings market remained strong ahead of RRA
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK rental market showed continued strength in April, with demand for rental properties remaining robust as the Renters’ Reform Bill progressed through Parliament. Industry data suggests landlords and tenants alike were adapting to potential changes whilst maintaining steady market activity.

Rental demand holds steady

Recent market analysis indicates rental enquiries and tenancy agreements remained at elevated levels during April. This sustained demand comes despite ongoing discussions about rental reforms, suggesting many renters are prioritising securing properties over waiting for legislative changes.

Several factors may be contributing to this resilience:

  • Continuing housing shortages in popular rental areas
  • Strong employment figures supporting tenants' ability to pay
  • Landlords maintaining properties to attract quality tenants

Preparing for rental reforms

With the Renters’ Reform Bill expected to introduce significant changes to the private rental sector, both landlords and letting agents appear to be taking a proactive approach. Many are reviewing their portfolios and tenancy agreements in anticipation of the new legislation.

Property professionals might consider:

  • Reviewing existing tenancy terms to ensure compliance
  • Assessing property standards against potential new requirements
  • Maintaining open communication with current tenants about any changes

The coming months will likely see further adjustments as the rental market adapts to both economic conditions and regulatory developments. Both landlords and tenants may benefit from staying informed about how these changes could affect their rental agreements and rights.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK rental market showed continued strength in April, with demand for rental properties remaining robust as the Renters’ Reform Bill progressed through Parliament. Industry data suggests landlords and tenants alike were adapting to potential changes whilst maintaining steady market activity.

Rental demand holds steady

Recent market analysis indicates rental enquiries and tenancy agreements remained at elevated levels during April. This sustained demand comes despite ongoing discussions about rental reforms, suggesting many renters are prioritising securing properties over waiting for legislative changes.

Several factors may be contributing to this resilience:

  • Continuing housing shortages in popular rental areas
  • Strong employment figures supporting tenants' ability to pay
  • Landlords maintaining properties to attract quality tenants

Preparing for rental reforms

With the Renters’ Reform Bill expected to introduce significant changes to the private rental sector, both landlords and letting agents appear to be taking a proactive approach. Many are reviewing their portfolios and tenancy agreements in anticipation of the new legislation.

Property professionals might consider:

  • Reviewing existing tenancy terms to ensure compliance
  • Assessing property standards against potential new requirements
  • Maintaining open communication with current tenants about any changes

The coming months will likely see further adjustments as the rental market adapts to both economic conditions and regulatory developments. Both landlords and tenants may benefit from staying informed about how these changes could affect their rental agreements and rights.

item.title: Planning applications hit 14-year low
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The number of planning applications submitted in the UK has dropped to its lowest level in 14 years, according to recent data. This decline highlights ongoing challenges within the planning system, including delays and uncertainty, which are impacting the delivery of new housing projects across the country.

What’s Behind the Decline?

Several factors are believed to have contributed to the significant drop in planning applications. Lengthy processing times and bureaucratic hurdles are deterring developers and homeowners from submitting proposals. Additionally, economic uncertainty and rising costs in the construction sector have made some projects less viable, leading to a slowdown in applications.

Local authorities are also facing resource shortages, which has resulted in delays in assessing and approving applications. This backlog creates a ripple effect, discouraging further submissions and exacerbating the housing shortage in many areas.

Implications for the Housing Market

The decline in planning applications could have far-reaching consequences for the UK housing market. With fewer new homes being approved, the already limited housing supply may shrink further, potentially driving up property prices and rental costs. This could make it increasingly difficult for first-time buyers to enter the market and for renters to find affordable accommodation.

For homeowners and property investors, this trend may signal a need to reassess their strategies. Those looking to extend or renovate their properties might face longer waiting times, while investors could find fewer opportunities for new developments.

As the planning system continues to face challenges, addressing these issues will be crucial to ensuring a steady supply of housing in the UK. Stakeholders, including policymakers and developers, may need to explore solutions to streamline processes and encourage more applications in the future.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The number of planning applications submitted in the UK has dropped to its lowest level in 14 years, according to recent data. This decline highlights ongoing challenges within the planning system, including delays and uncertainty, which are impacting the delivery of new housing projects across the country.

What’s Behind the Decline?

Several factors are believed to have contributed to the significant drop in planning applications. Lengthy processing times and bureaucratic hurdles are deterring developers and homeowners from submitting proposals. Additionally, economic uncertainty and rising costs in the construction sector have made some projects less viable, leading to a slowdown in applications.

Local authorities are also facing resource shortages, which has resulted in delays in assessing and approving applications. This backlog creates a ripple effect, discouraging further submissions and exacerbating the housing shortage in many areas.

Implications for the Housing Market

The decline in planning applications could have far-reaching consequences for the UK housing market. With fewer new homes being approved, the already limited housing supply may shrink further, potentially driving up property prices and rental costs. This could make it increasingly difficult for first-time buyers to enter the market and for renters to find affordable accommodation.

For homeowners and property investors, this trend may signal a need to reassess their strategies. Those looking to extend or renovate their properties might face longer waiting times, while investors could find fewer opportunities for new developments.

As the planning system continues to face challenges, addressing these issues will be crucial to ensuring a steady supply of housing in the UK. Stakeholders, including policymakers and developers, may need to explore solutions to streamline processes and encourage more applications in the future.

item.title: Letting agents urged to act now ahead of latest Renters’ Rights Act deadline
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Letting agents and landlords across England and Wales are being reminded of an important deadline under the Renters’ Rights Act, with just weeks remaining to distribute mandatory tenant information sheets. Industry experts warn that failure to comply by 31 May could result in substantial fines of up to £7,000 per breach.

Key Requirements for Landlords and Agents

The legislation requires all tenants named on assured or assured shorthold tenancy agreements that began before 1 May 2026 to receive the official Renters’ Rights Act Information Sheet. Propertymark, the professional body for the property sector, has clarified that simply providing a website link does not meet the legal obligation. The document must be supplied either as a physical copy or an electronic attachment sent directly to tenants.

Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, emphasised: "Agents should immediately review their tenancy records to ensure every named tenant receives this documentation in the correct format. With the deadline approaching, prompt action could help avoid penalties while demonstrating compliance with these significant legislative changes."

Understanding the Wider Reforms

This requirement forms part of broader reforms introduced by the Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force earlier this month. The legislation has brought substantial changes to the private rental sector, including:

  • The abolition of fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies
  • New procedures for rent increases
  • Enhanced protections against unfair evictions
  • Stricter regulations around property conditions

Industry observers note that these changes mark one of the most significant overhauls of private rental sector legislation in decades. The information sheet requirement serves to ensure tenants fully understand their new rights under the law.

With the compliance deadline fast approaching, letting professionals may wish to prioritise this administrative task to avoid potential penalties. The property sector continues to adapt to these reforms whilst navigating other challenges in the current rental market.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Letting agents and landlords across England and Wales are being reminded of an important deadline under the Renters’ Rights Act, with just weeks remaining to distribute mandatory tenant information sheets. Industry experts warn that failure to comply by 31 May could result in substantial fines of up to £7,000 per breach.

Key Requirements for Landlords and Agents

The legislation requires all tenants named on assured or assured shorthold tenancy agreements that began before 1 May 2026 to receive the official Renters’ Rights Act Information Sheet. Propertymark, the professional body for the property sector, has clarified that simply providing a website link does not meet the legal obligation. The document must be supplied either as a physical copy or an electronic attachment sent directly to tenants.

Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, emphasised: "Agents should immediately review their tenancy records to ensure every named tenant receives this documentation in the correct format. With the deadline approaching, prompt action could help avoid penalties while demonstrating compliance with these significant legislative changes."

Understanding the Wider Reforms

This requirement forms part of broader reforms introduced by the Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force earlier this month. The legislation has brought substantial changes to the private rental sector, including:

  • The abolition of fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies
  • New procedures for rent increases
  • Enhanced protections against unfair evictions
  • Stricter regulations around property conditions

Industry observers note that these changes mark one of the most significant overhauls of private rental sector legislation in decades. The information sheet requirement serves to ensure tenants fully understand their new rights under the law.

With the compliance deadline fast approaching, letting professionals may wish to prioritise this administrative task to avoid potential penalties. The property sector continues to adapt to these reforms whilst navigating other challenges in the current rental market.

item.title: Seaside property hotspots defy market slowdown as prices surge by up to 11%
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst the UK housing market has faced challenges over the past year, with average asking prices dipping by 0.3%, certain coastal towns are defying the trend. Seaside property hotspots are experiencing significant price growth, driven by sustained demand for coastal living. Bootle in Merseyside leads the way, with asking prices surging by 11% year-on-year, according to recent analysis by Rightmove.

Coastal Towns Outperforming the National Market

Alongside Bootle, other coastal locations are also posting strong price increases. Crosby, near Liverpool, and Penarth in South Glamorgan have both seen asking prices rise by 9% annually. Llantwit Major in South Glamorgan recorded an 8% increase, while Llanelli in Carmarthenshire saw a 7% uplift. These figures highlight the continued appeal of coastal living, even as the broader market faces headwinds.

One key factor driving this trend is the relative affordability of many seaside towns. Nine of the ten fastest-growing coastal locations still have average asking prices below the national benchmark of £378,304. Over four-fifths of the more than 100 coastal areas analysed remain cheaper than the UK average, making them attractive options for buyers seeking a coastal lifestyle without the premium prices of more established hotspots.

From Affordable to Luxury: The Coastal Price Spectrum

The coastal property market spans a wide price range, catering to diverse budgets. At the luxury end, Sandbanks in Poole retains its status as Britain’s most expensive seaside location, with an average asking price exceeding £1.1 million. In contrast, Peterlee in County Durham is the most affordable coastal town, where homes come to market at an average of £120,657.

Other coastal towns offering value include Barrow-In-Furness in Cumbria, with average prices of £185,169, and Bangor in Gwynedd, where homes are priced at £220,622. These locations provide opportunities for buyers to enjoy coastal living at a more accessible price point, contributing to their popularity and price growth.

The resilience of seaside property markets underscores the enduring appeal of coastal living, offering a blend of affordability, lifestyle, and investment potential. For those considering a move to the coast, these trends highlight the importance of exploring a range of locations to find the right balance of value and lifestyle.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst the UK housing market has faced challenges over the past year, with average asking prices dipping by 0.3%, certain coastal towns are defying the trend. Seaside property hotspots are experiencing significant price growth, driven by sustained demand for coastal living. Bootle in Merseyside leads the way, with asking prices surging by 11% year-on-year, according to recent analysis by Rightmove.

Coastal Towns Outperforming the National Market

Alongside Bootle, other coastal locations are also posting strong price increases. Crosby, near Liverpool, and Penarth in South Glamorgan have both seen asking prices rise by 9% annually. Llantwit Major in South Glamorgan recorded an 8% increase, while Llanelli in Carmarthenshire saw a 7% uplift. These figures highlight the continued appeal of coastal living, even as the broader market faces headwinds.

One key factor driving this trend is the relative affordability of many seaside towns. Nine of the ten fastest-growing coastal locations still have average asking prices below the national benchmark of £378,304. Over four-fifths of the more than 100 coastal areas analysed remain cheaper than the UK average, making them attractive options for buyers seeking a coastal lifestyle without the premium prices of more established hotspots.

From Affordable to Luxury: The Coastal Price Spectrum

The coastal property market spans a wide price range, catering to diverse budgets. At the luxury end, Sandbanks in Poole retains its status as Britain’s most expensive seaside location, with an average asking price exceeding £1.1 million. In contrast, Peterlee in County Durham is the most affordable coastal town, where homes come to market at an average of £120,657.

Other coastal towns offering value include Barrow-In-Furness in Cumbria, with average prices of £185,169, and Bangor in Gwynedd, where homes are priced at £220,622. These locations provide opportunities for buyers to enjoy coastal living at a more accessible price point, contributing to their popularity and price growth.

The resilience of seaside property markets underscores the enduring appeal of coastal living, offering a blend of affordability, lifestyle, and investment potential. For those considering a move to the coast, these trends highlight the importance of exploring a range of locations to find the right balance of value and lifestyle.

item.title: Knight Frank raises ‘red flags’ over government’s leasehold reform plans
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government’s leasehold reform proposals have sparked debate across the property sector, with experts highlighting potential risks to the housing market. Whilst the overarching aim to empower leaseholders and transition away from traditional leasehold models has been welcomed, concerns remain about the finer details of the plans. Knight Frank, a leading estate agency, has identified several "red flags" that could lead to unintended consequences for leaseholders, freeholders, and lenders alike.

Concerns Over Commonhold Conversion

One of the most contentious proposals is the suggestion that leaseholders could convert a building to commonhold with the support of just 50% of owners. Knight Frank warns that this threshold could force significant changes on those who oppose the move or choose not to participate. This scenario might lead to disputes and complicate ownership structures, potentially deterring leaseholders from pursuing enfranchisement altogether.

Jeremy Dharmasena, Head of Leasehold Reform & Litigation at Knight Frank, commented, "Whilst we support the government’s ambition to empower leaseholders fairly, there are clear concerns within the proposals. A 50% threshold could effectively bounce non-participating leaseholders into commonhold, leaving them with little control over their property’s future value and resale appeal."

Ground Rent Caps and Retrospective Changes

Another area of concern is the proposed £250 cap on ground rents, which some argue clashes with existing provisions in the Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024. Knight Frank suggests that a 40-year period to reduce ground rents would be a fairer solution, as the suggested 20-year timeframe risks being overly punitive to freeholders. Additionally, retrospective changes to lease terms could send mixed messages to investors, potentially undermining confidence in the market.

Dharmasena added, "There is a misconception that commonhold will resolve all issues for leaseholders. In reality, it does not address the rising costs of maintaining a building, which will still fall on residents under a commonhold model. Policymakers must carefully consider these factors to avoid unintended consequences."

The Importance of Balanced Reform

Whilst leasehold reform is widely seen as necessary, experts emphasise the importance of balancing the interests of all parties involved. The transition to commonhold must be carefully managed to avoid disrupting confidence in the apartment market. Knight Frank’s warnings highlight the need for policymakers to address these concerns before finalising the legislation.

As the government moves forward with its plans, homeowners may wish to stay informed about how these reforms could impact their properties. Borrowers might also consider seeking professional advice to navigate the potential changes and understand their implications fully.

Ultimately, whilst the drive to empower leaseholders is commendable, the devil is in the detail. Ensuring a fair and balanced approach will be crucial to achieving lasting reform without unintended consequences for the housing market.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government’s leasehold reform proposals have sparked debate across the property sector, with experts highlighting potential risks to the housing market. Whilst the overarching aim to empower leaseholders and transition away from traditional leasehold models has been welcomed, concerns remain about the finer details of the plans. Knight Frank, a leading estate agency, has identified several "red flags" that could lead to unintended consequences for leaseholders, freeholders, and lenders alike.

Concerns Over Commonhold Conversion

One of the most contentious proposals is the suggestion that leaseholders could convert a building to commonhold with the support of just 50% of owners. Knight Frank warns that this threshold could force significant changes on those who oppose the move or choose not to participate. This scenario might lead to disputes and complicate ownership structures, potentially deterring leaseholders from pursuing enfranchisement altogether.

Jeremy Dharmasena, Head of Leasehold Reform & Litigation at Knight Frank, commented, "Whilst we support the government’s ambition to empower leaseholders fairly, there are clear concerns within the proposals. A 50% threshold could effectively bounce non-participating leaseholders into commonhold, leaving them with little control over their property’s future value and resale appeal."

Ground Rent Caps and Retrospective Changes

Another area of concern is the proposed £250 cap on ground rents, which some argue clashes with existing provisions in the Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024. Knight Frank suggests that a 40-year period to reduce ground rents would be a fairer solution, as the suggested 20-year timeframe risks being overly punitive to freeholders. Additionally, retrospective changes to lease terms could send mixed messages to investors, potentially undermining confidence in the market.

Dharmasena added, "There is a misconception that commonhold will resolve all issues for leaseholders. In reality, it does not address the rising costs of maintaining a building, which will still fall on residents under a commonhold model. Policymakers must carefully consider these factors to avoid unintended consequences."

The Importance of Balanced Reform

Whilst leasehold reform is widely seen as necessary, experts emphasise the importance of balancing the interests of all parties involved. The transition to commonhold must be carefully managed to avoid disrupting confidence in the apartment market. Knight Frank’s warnings highlight the need for policymakers to address these concerns before finalising the legislation.

As the government moves forward with its plans, homeowners may wish to stay informed about how these reforms could impact their properties. Borrowers might also consider seeking professional advice to navigate the potential changes and understand their implications fully.

Ultimately, whilst the drive to empower leaseholders is commendable, the devil is in the detail. Ensuring a fair and balanced approach will be crucial to achieving lasting reform without unintended consequences for the housing market.

item.title: Two weeks to go until Propertymark One 2026
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

With just two weeks remaining until Propertymark One 2026, excitement is mounting for what is set to be one of the property industry’s most significant events of the year. Taking place at ExCeL London, this conference will bring together property professionals from across the UK for a day of insightful discussions, networking opportunities, and expert-led debates. The event promises to offer valuable perspectives on the challenges and opportunities shaping the property sector.

Key Speakers and Topics

Propertymark One 2026 will feature an impressive lineup of speakers from diverse fields, including politics, business, economics, and technology. Attendees will have the chance to hear from Angela Rayner, former deputy prime minister, who will participate in a live Q&A session focusing on housing policy and reform. Sir Mel Stride is set to share his views on the economic outlook and property taxation, offering valuable insights for industry professionals.

Other notable speakers include Karren Brady, who will discuss leadership and business growth, and political commentator Daisy McAndrew. Technology presenter Lara Lewington will also take the stage to explore the increasing role of artificial intelligence in the business world. These sessions aim to equip delegates with the knowledge and tools needed to navigate a rapidly evolving market.

Networking and Industry Collaboration

Beyond the conference programme, Propertymark One 2026 provides a prime opportunity for agents to reconnect with colleagues, exchange ideas, and build valuable industry connections. The event’s exhibition floor is already sold out, with over 80 leading suppliers confirmed to showcase their products and services. This vibrant environment is designed to foster collaboration and innovation within the property sector.

Jason Lee, head of commercial at Propertymark, highlighted the importance of the event, stating, “Propertymark One is about bringing the sector together at a pivotal time. It offers agents access to the insight, tools, and connections they need to thrive in a fast-moving market.” With final tickets now limited, those considering attending are encouraged to secure their place promptly to avoid missing out.

For property professionals seeking to stay ahead in a competitive and dynamic industry, Propertymark One 2026 represents an unmissable opportunity to gain valuable insights and strengthen professional networks.

Taking place at ExCeL London, this conference will bring together property professionals from across the UK for a day of insightful discussions, networking opportunities, and expert-led debates.
